title: How City Rats Are Becoming Resistant to Poison
gist: A Rutgers study of NE-US rodents finds widespread genetic resistance to rodenticides — 84% of house mice and 35% of rats carry survival mutations (mostly Vkorc1) — helping explain why urban rodent populations are rising despite billions spent on eradication.

insights.claim: 84 percent of sampled house mice and 35 percent of Norway rats in the northeastern US carry at least one genetic mutation, mostly in the gene Vkorc1, that lets them survive rodent poisons.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Chemical rodent control is losing efficacy; signals demand shift toward new pest-control chemistries or non-chemical methods.
insights.quote: 84 percent of house mice carried at least one genetic mutation that allowed them to survive rodent poisons.

insights.claim: Urban rodent populations are rising worldwide despite billions of dollars spent annually on eradication.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A large, growing, structurally underserved pest-control market where incumbent products are failing.
insights.quote: despite billions of dollars spent annually on eradication, research shows that rodents are on the rise in cities worldwide
insights.locator: Yet, despite billions of dollars spent

insights.claim: Rats are evolving resistance faster than current eradication methods can keep up, per the framing of a Pest Science Management study from Rutgers entomologists.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Resistance evolution is the structural driver; watch biotech/agtech pest-control innovators positioned to replace failing rodenticides.
insights.quote: They’re evolving faster than we can stop them
